Senator Bob wilson 
California 3tate Senate 
State Capitol 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Dear Senator Wilson: 
/l- '/9- 77 
December 13, 1979 
Thank you for your letter of November 16, 1979, r 
uesting 
advice with respect to the campaign disclosure provisions of 
the Political Reform ~ct. Government Code Sections 84100 -
84400. 
I hope the tollowing advice provided pursuant to 
Government Code Section 831l4(b) is helpful. 
You have requested advice concerning the reporting of 
contributions involving an inter~ediary. Specifically, you 
have asked the following questions: 
(1) 
What is the detinition of an intermediary? 
(2) 
Is it necessary to list {a PAC as] an intermediary 
when reporting contributions from a PAC and when 
the I.D. number is used? 
(3) 
What is [your] responsibility in reporting inter-
mediaries when they do not aivulge such information 
to [you}? 
WHAT IS llHE DEFINITIO~: OF AN -INTERMEDIARY"? 
No explicit definition of the term -intermediary· is 
contained in either the Act or Com~ission regulations. 
However, because Government Code Section 84302 uses the term 
in the context of requiring reporting of the true donor of 
contributions, the Commission has given the term a functional 
definition consistent with the disclosure purposes of the 
Act. 
Government Code Section 84302 provides, in pertinent 
part: 


3e~ator ob Wilson 
Cecember 13, 1979 
Pag e :'''10 
No person shall make a contribution on behalf of 
another, or while acting as the intermediary or 
agent of another, without uisclosing to the recipi-
ent of the contribution both his own full name ana 
street address, occupation, and the name of his 
employer •••• 
The recipient of the contribution 
shall include in his campaign statement the full 
name and street address, occupation, and the name 
of the employer, if any, or the principal place of 
business if self-employed, of both tne intermediary 
and the contributor. 
In view of the purpose of the section to require identi-
fication of the true donor, the Commission has said in past 
aavice letters that someone stands as an intermediary in 
relation to a contributor when, without disclosure of the 
identity of the contributor, the intermediary would be con-
sidered the source of the contribution by the recipient. 
In 
such cases, the intermediary must disclose to the recipient 
that it is not -his· contribution, but that of another. 
What this functicnal approach eliminates from the definition 
of intermeaiary is the person who merely delivers a contribution. 
Thus, when a person makes a contribution by cheCK payable to 
the candidate, the delivery of the check by a third party 
does not make the third party an intermediary. 
This would 
be the case even if the check were made payable to the third 
party and endorsed by the third party to the candidate. 
In 
both cases, what makes this merely delivery is the tact that 
the drawer of the check is revealed as the true donor. 
However, when a check is payable to the third party, but 
earmarked by the contrioutor for a specific candidate, the 
third party becomes an intermediary when the contribution is 
made by means of his check. 
IS IT NECESSARY TO LIST AN 
INTER~lE!HARY 
WHEN REPORTING CONTRIBUTIONS FROM A PAC 
AND WHEN THE 1.0. NCMBER IS LISTED? 
The answer .to the question depends upon whether, accoraing 
to the functional approach described above, the PAC is acting 
as an inte 
iary in making the contributions. 
Accordingly, 
where a contribution is made to the PAC to be distributed at 
the discretion of the PAC, the PAC is the true donor oecause 
its discretion deteroines the recipient. 
uowever, woen, as 
in the situation described in the preceding section, the 
cicncr earmarks the contribution, the PAC is an inter~e~iary. 


Senator £00 wilson 
December 13, 1~79 
Pa;3e Three 
YOUR RESPONSIBILITY' IN REPORTHm DiTEIDtEDIARIES 
hHEr:r THEY DO NOT DIVULGE SUCH 
!NFOR~1ATION TO YOU 
Government Code 3ection d4302 requires a recipient of a 
contribution from an intermediary to report both the inter-
mediary and the contributor. 
The inter~ediary also has an 
lnaependent duty to disclose his acting as an intermediary. 
When a person does not disclose to the recipient of a con-
tribution that he is acting as an intermediary and there is 
no reason tor a recipient to believe that the prima facie 
contributor might be an intermediary, there is no liability 
for failure to report the intermediary. 
This standard of 
liaDility is cased upon Government Code Section 91004, which 
creates liability for both intentional and negligent violations 
of reporting requirements, and upon the wreasonaole diligence-
reporting standard of Goverlli~ent Code Sections ~l004 ana 
04209. 
I nope that I have answered your questiono. 
If you 
desire further information or additional clarification, 
please do not hesitate to call me. 
:plh 
Very truly yours, 
Thomas M. Sobel 
Chief 
Legal Division

sections.conclusion: No explicit definition of the term 'intermediary' is contained in either the Act or Commission regulations. However, the Commission has given the term a functional definition: someone stands as an intermediary when, without disclosure of the identity of the contributor, the intermediary would be considered the source of the contribution by the recipient. A mere delivery person is not an intermediary. Whether a PAC is an intermediary depends on whether it acts with discretion (making it the true donor) or whether the contributor earmarks the contribution (making the PAC an intermediary). When an intermediary does not disclose and there is no reason for the recipient to believe the prima facie contributor might be an intermediary, there is no liability for failure to report the intermediary.
sections.question_synthetic: The requester seeks clarification on three matters: the definition of an intermediary under campaign disclosure law, whether PACs must be listed as intermediaries when their I.D. number is used, and what reporting obligations apply when intermediaries fail to disclose their status.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC provides a functional definition of intermediary based on whether the recipient would consider the person the true source without disclosure of the actual contributor. A PAC is an intermediary only when the original donor earmarks the contribution; otherwise, the PAC is the true donor. Recipients have no liability for unreported intermediaries if the intermediary failed to disclose and there was no reason to suspect intermediary status.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document includes main FPPC advice letter dated December 13, 1979, plus a follow-up request from law firm Sacks & Zweig dated October 3, 1983 requesting clarification on a related scenario. Primary confidence based on main advice letter; confidence reduced slightly due to unclear/damaged text in some OCR sections and the appended follow-up inquiry.

CONCLUSION: No explicit definition of the term 'intermediary' is contained in either the Act or Commission regulations. However, the Commission has given the term a functional definition: someone stands as an intermediary when, without disclosure of the identity of the contributor, the intermediary would be considered the source of the contribution by the recipient. A mere delivery person is not an intermediary. Whether a PAC is an intermediary depends on whether it acts with discretion (making it the true donor) or whether the contributor earmarks the contribution (making the PAC an intermediary). When an intermediary does not disclose and there is no reason for the recipient to believe the prima facie contributor might be an intermediary, there is no liability for failure to report the intermediary.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises Senator Bob Wilson on campaign disclosure requirements regarding intermediaries under the Political Reform Act, defining the term functionally based on whether the recipient would view someone as the true donor, and explaining when PACs are intermediaries and the reporting obligations when intermediaries fail to disclose.
